content.text:
I see the issue. Looking at the test, it expects that when an already unmodifiable trie is passed to `TrieUtils.unmodifiableTrie()`, it should return the same instance. This means there should be logic somewhere that detects if the input is already unmodifiable and returns it as-is, instead of wrapping it again.

However, I notice that the `unmodifiableTrie` method is defined inside the `UnmodifiableTrie` class, not in `TrieUtils` class. Let me check if there's a `TrieUtils` class:

content.content: Found 2 matches

content.text: Let me check the TrieUtils class that contains the factory method:

Now I understand the issue. The test expects that if we pass an already unmodifiable trie to `TrieUtils.unmodifiableTrie()`, it should return the same instance. But currently, the `UnmodifiableTrie.unmodifiableTrie()` method always creates a new wrapper regardless of whether the input is already an unmodifiable trie.

Let me check the exact failure by running the specific test with more details:

input.description: Compiling and running the specific test to see detailed failure

content.content:
Running ant (compile.tests)................................................ OK
Running ant (run.dev.tests)................................................ OK
Failing tests: 1
  - org.apache.commons.collections4.TrieUtilsTest::testUnmodifiableTrie
Running ant (compile)...................................................... OK
Running ant (compile.tests)................................................ OK

content.text: Let me check if there are other similar utility classes to see how they handle this situation. Let me also check the interface or parent traits:

content.content: Found 1 match

content.text: Let me look at other unmodifiable implementations in this codebase to see the pattern:

content.content: Found 40 matching file(s)

content.text: Let me look at the UnmodifiableMap implementation to see how it handles this pattern:

Perfect! I found the issue. In the `UnmodifiableMap.unmodifiableMap()` method, there's a check: `if (map instanceof Unmodifiable)`. If the input map is already unmodifiable, it returns it directly instead of wrapping it again.

However, in the `UnmodifiableTrie.unmodifiableTrie()` method, there's no such check. It always creates a new `UnmodifiableTrie` wrapper. This is the bug!

Let me also check the Unmodifiable interface to understand the pattern:

content.content:
/* * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership. * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0 * (the "License"); you may not use this file except in compliance with * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at * * http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 * * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software * distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied. * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and * limitations under the License. */ package org.apache.commons.collections4; /** * Marker interface for collections, maps and iterators that are unmodifiable. *

* This interface enables testing such as: * 

 * if (coll instanceof Unmodifiable) {
 *   coll = new ArrayList(coll);
 * }
 * // now we know coll is modifiable
 *
* Of course all this only works if you use the Unmodifiable classes defined * in this library. If you use the JDK unmodifiable class via {@code java.util Collections} * then the interface won't be there. * * @since 3.0 * @version $Id$ */ public interface Unmodifiable { // marker interface - no methods to implement }

content.text: Perfect! Now I understand the issue completely. The `UnmodifiableTrie.unmodifiableTrie()` method should check if the input trie is already an instance of `Unmodifiable` before creating a new wrapper.
